resume:
DANIEL ORTEGA
Operations Manager — Fulfillment & Distribution
Reno, NV | daniel.ortega.ops@example.com | open to relocation

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Operations professional with seven years spanning e-commerce fulfillment, last-mile coordination, and warehouse process design. I have run shifts, owned regional throughput targets, and led cross-functional projects connecting WMS data to staffing decisions. I am most comfortable on the floor with a clipboard and equally comfortable in a planning meeting defending a labor model. My north star is flow: getting units from inbound dock to outbound truck with the least touches, the least dwell, and the fewest safety incidents. I believe operations is a people business first and a process business second, because even the best-designed process fails if the team on the floor does not trust it.

PROFESSIONAL EXPERIENCE

QuickShip Logistics — Operations Manager, Reno Fulfillment Center (Mar 2021 – Present)
Reno FC is a 410,000 sq ft facility shipping roughly 95,000 units on a peak day across two shifts and about 280 associates at peak. I own outbound operations: pick, pack, ship, and the problem-solve queue.
- Inherited an outbound area missing its units-per-hour (UPH) target by a wide margin during the 2021 holiday ramp. I ran a two-week time study across pick paths, found that pickers were walking excess distance because of a slotting scheme that put high-velocity SKUs at the far end of the module, and partnered with the inventory control team to re-slot the top 200 movers closer to the pack stations. Outbound UPH improved meaningfully over the following quarter and held through the next peak.
- Redesigned the shift-handover process. Previously, the outgoing shift lead briefed the incoming lead verbally and a lot was lost. I introduced a one-page standardized handover doc covering open exceptions, down equipment, and staffing gaps, and I made the two leads walk the floor together for the first fifteen minutes of overlap. Exception carryover between shifts dropped noticeably and the morning lead stopped starting the day blind.
- Built a daily labor-planning huddle that pulls forecasted volume from the WMS the night before and maps it to a flex staffing plan, so we stopped over-calling temp labor on light days and under-staffing on surge days. This was the project I am most proud of because it touched both cost and morale.
- Chair the facility safety committee. We went from a frustrating run of recordable incidents tied to manual lifting in the returns area to a much calmer state after I worked with the safety specialist to add lift-assist devices and rewrite the standard work for the heaviest totes.
- Manage five area supervisors and coordinate daily with inbound, inventory control, and the regional transportation team on trailer scheduling and dock-door assignment.

MetroParcel — Shift Supervisor, Sacramento Sort Center (Jun 2018 – Feb 2021)
Sacramento sort was a high-tempo parcel hub. I supervised the night sort, about 60 associates, responsible for unloading inbound trailers, sorting to outbound destinations, and meeting a hard dispatch cutoff every night.
- Owned the dispatch cutoff for my sort aisle. Missing the cutoff meant a parcel missed its service commitment, so the whole shift was a race against a clock that did not move. I learned to read the inbound trailer manifest early and pre-position labor at the aisles that were about to get slammed.
- Cross-trained associates across unload, sort, and load so I could shift bodies to the bottleneck in real time instead of letting one station drown while another stood idle.
- Ran the daily standup for my shift and handled the bulk of associate coaching, attendance conversations, and the occasional escalation that needed HR.
- Acted as relief operations manager during my supervisor's medical leave for about three months, which is when I got my first taste of owning a P&L line and sitting in the weekly regional review.

GreenLeaf Grocery — Inventory & Receiving Associate, then Lead (Aug 2016 – May 2018)
My start in operations. Began on the receiving dock checking in deliveries against POs, moved up to receiving lead within a year.
- Reconciled inbound deliveries against purchase orders and chased down discrepancies with vendors and the buying team.
- Reorganized the backroom storage layout so that high-turn ambient goods were nearest the floor access door, which cut restocking trips for the night crew.
- Trained new receiving associates on the handheld scanners and the receiving SOP.

KEY PROJECTS
- Slotting Optimization (QuickShip, 2021–2022): Led the analysis and the cross-functional rollout to re-slot fast movers. Coordinated with inventory control, IT for the WMS location updates, and the floor leads for the physical move executed over two weekends without stopping outbound.
- Flex Labor Model (QuickShip, 2022): Built the forecast-to-staffing huddle and the simple spreadsheet model behind it. Worked with the regional WFM analyst to validate the volume forecast.
- Returns Area Safety Redesign (QuickShip, 2023): Chaired the effort to reduce manual-lifting incidents in returns through lift-assist equipment and rewritten standard work.

SKILLS
WMS (Manhattan, and exposure to a homegrown system at MetroParcel), labor planning and flex staffing, time studies and work measurement, slotting and warehouse layout, shift management, safety committee leadership, exception and problem-solve queue management, Excel labor modeling, basic SQL for pulling WMS extracts, root-cause problem solving, associate coaching and development.

CERTIFICATIONS
Lean Six Sigma Green Belt; OSHA 30; Powered Industrial Truck (sit-down and reach) certified trainer.

EDUCATION
B.S., Supply Chain Management — University of Nevada. Active member of the campus APICS chapter.

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR
A mid-level operations role where I own a measurable slice of throughput and a real team, in a culture that treats the floor associate as the customer of the process. I want to keep building labor models and slotting strategies but at a larger scale, and eventually grow into a senior or area-director track. I am energized by peak season, by the puzzle of matching variable labor to variable volume, and by the moment a struggling shift finally clicks because the standard work and the staffing finally line up.
jd: Cascade Fulfillment is hiring an Operations Manager to own outbound for our 350,000 sq ft Pacific Northwest distribution center, shipping up to 70,000 units on a peak day across two shifts. Reporting to the Site Director, you will own pick/pack/ship throughput, a team of four to six area supervisors, the daily labor plan, and the outbound portion of the site's safety record. We are specifically looking for someone who has personally led a measurable throughput improvement — not just participated in one. In the interview we will ask you to walk through one project end to end: the baseline, the root cause you found, the specific intervention you drove, who you had to influence, and the quantified result. We want a candidate who can stay precise under pressure and attribute clearly what they did versus what the team did. Lean tools, WMS labor modeling, and a genuine safety-first instinct are expected.

history.q: Daniel, give me the shape of your current role at QuickShip — facility size, what you own, team size.
Your resume highlights a slotting optimization. Start at the beginning: how did you know slotting was the problem and not, say, picker training?
You said you re-slotted the top 200 movers. How did you decide it was 200 and not 50 or 500?
Who actually had to sign off and execute that move, and what was your specific role versus inventory control's?
You mentioned UPH 'improved meaningfully.' Give me the baseline number and the after number.
Let's talk about the flex labor model. What was the actual mechanism — how does volume from the WMS turn into a staffing decision?
When the model said 'light day' but a supervisor wanted more bodies anyway, how did you handle the disagreement?
On the returns safety work — walk me through the single incident that triggered the redesign.
Okay, take the slotting project specifically. In one clean narrative: baseline, root cause, the intervention you personally drove, and the quantified result. Give me the numbers.

candidate_last_answer: Okay, yeah, so the slotting thing — sorry, let me think, there's a lot there. So basically, um, the baseline, I mean it was bad, the UPH was just not where it needed to be, and honestly peak was insane that year, we were all just drowning, and I remember one night the conveyor went down for like two hours which didn't help anything. So, root cause — well, walking, the pickers were walking too much, that was kind of the big thing, although also I think morale was low and that matters, like you can't separate the human element, and we had a lot of temps that peak who didn't know the building. And the re-slot, right, so we moved the fast movers up, the IC team was great about it, and it definitely helped, I want to say it went up by a decent chunk, like — oh, and I should mention we also fixed a labeling issue at the same time which probably contributed. Sorry, what was the specific number you wanted again?

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: it went up by a decent chunk

expected-yield: a single committed baseline UPH number and after number (with the time window), and clean isolation of the slotting effect from the confounding labeling fix and morale factors the candidate keeps drifting into
